<list>Environmental evaluation of a Portuguese dairy company. 
Ten impact categories have been assessed in detail. 
Raw milk production in the dairy farm is the main hot spot in almost all the categories. 
On-site emissions derived from dairy factory are remarkable together with the packages and energy requirements production. 
Sensitivity assessment of the allocation approach was performed to identify differences on the environmental results.</list>

4: The food sector and therefore, the dairy sector, is an important source of environmental impact in terms of depletion of resources and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (Meissner Schau and Magerholm Fet, 2008; Garnett, 2009; Popp et al., 2010; Iribarren et al., 2011). There- fore, improvements throughout the production line must be proposed for the steps with highest environmental burdens (hot spots) (Berlin, 2002; Berlin et al., 2008).
In recent years and due to the growing concern by mankind concerning environmental issues, the interest in assessing environmental

effects such as the carbon footprint of dairy products has increased. In fact, there are some supply chains such as Tesco in the United Kingdom and Casino in France, whose milk and dairy products are carrying carbon la- bels (Flysjö et al., 2011).

The dairy industry is an example of an industrial process characterised by the connection of different production subsystems: agriculture, dairy farming and dairy factory (Hogaas Eide, 2002; Hospido et al., 2003). Korsström and Lampi (2008) established that the main environmental impacts derived from the dairy industries are related to water consump- tion, energy requirements and generation of wastewater with high organ- ic load. In fact, dairy industries show high water requirements mainly derived from the cleaning operations of equipment and production facil- ities as well as from cooling operations (Ramirez et al., 2006). Several studies have examined the energy requirements in the European dairy sector (Hogaas Eide, 2002; Sonesson and Berlin, 2003; Ramirez et al., 2006; Korsström and Lampi, 2008; Demeter et al., 2009), where special

attention was paid to countries such as Sweden, France, Germany, The Netherlands and the United Kingdom. High energy requirements are required usually in the form of steam in the heating systems and cleaning, in the evaporation and drying processes as well as in the thermal process of pasteurisation, sterilisation and Ultra High Temperature (UHT) treatment (Ramirez et al., 2006). Moreover, electricity consumption is required in the operation of machineries, refrigeration, ventilation and lighting. Wastewater from dairies contains milk and other product wastes as well as cleaning chemicals. Thus, the effluents from this industrial sec- tor are characterised by having a high content of organic load, nitrogen and phosphorous (Korsström and Lampi, 2008).

Milk is the main dairy product of the European dairy sector and the basis for the production of other dairy products (cheese, cream, butter, yogurt, whey). Due to the high demand of milk for consump- tion and the relevance of the environmental effects derived from the dairy sector, it is required to investigate and determine the environ- mental consequences from its production. Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is an internationally standardized methodology which allows identifying the environmental consequences of the life cycle of a product by evaluating the potential environmental impacts over its whole life cycle production chain (ISO 14040, 2006). This methodolo- gy has been selected by several authors to evaluate the environmen- tal performance of dairy farms (Castanheira et al., 2010; Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000; Cederberg and Stadig, 2003; de Boer, 2003; O'Brien et al., 2012; Flysjö et al., 2011; Iribarren et al., 2011) as well as industrial liquid milk production (Hogaas Eide, 2002; Hospido et al., 2003; Demeter et al., 2009; Sonesson and Berlin, 2003; Fantin et al., 2012; Yan et al., 2011). Other dairy products assessed from an LCA perspective were cheese (Berlin, 2002), yogurts (Glende, 1997; Berlin and Sonesson, 2008) and milk powder (Ramirez et al., 2006).

To date, no environmental information is available regarding Portu- guese milk production, with milk being the most consumed dairy prod- uct in this country (INE, 2009). Only Castanheira et al. (2010) evaluated the environmental impacts derived from raw milk production at the farm gate in a typical Portuguese dairy farm. Consequently, it was decid- ed to undertake this study since the dairy sector is one of the most im- portant in the agro-food sector in Portugal, representing 12% of the business volume and 15% of market distribution (Portugal Foods, 2012).

In terms of the manufacturing of Portuguese dairy products, milk in its different forms (whole, semi-skimmed and skimmed milk) is the main product (~890,000 tonnes), followed by yogurts and cheese. In fact, these rates reflect the consumption trends (INE, 2012). More- over, UHT milk represents 93% of the total milk consumed in Portugal (Elliott, 2007).

This paper is focussed on UHT milk production in a Portuguese dairy factory representative of the state-of-art and presents a detailed life cycle inventory analysis and an environmental assessment of UHT milk production from cow milk.

9: The main objective of this study was to assess the environmental per- formance of the production of packed UHT milk (simple and cocoa) at the dairy factory gate in Portugal, in order to identify the environmental hot spots (the elements that have a high contribution to the environmen- tal burden). We will not only indentify the environmental impacts but also quantify the energy balance from a cradle-to-gate perspective. A dairy factory using a significant number of processes, technologies man- agement practices considered as Best Available Technologies (BATs) was selected to perform the assessment (Agência Portuguesa do Medio Ambiente, 2012). Fig. 1 shows the scheme of the production process under analysis with the corresponding involved processes. This factory uses as raw material 100% Portuguese raw milk.
10: The functional unit considered in this study was 1 kg of packaged energy-corrected milk (ECM), corrected based on fat and protein con- tent including co-products leaving the dairy-gate (i.e. milk, cream and butter). The selection of this functional unit is in agreement with other related dairy studies (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000; Casey and Holden, 2005; Bartl et al., 2011; Flysjö et al., 2011).

ECM is a correction factor generally used by the dairy industry (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000). It determines the amount of energy in the milk based on milk, fat and protein. In this study, it was calcu- lated following the formula described by Bartl et al. (2011), although considering the average fat and protein content of Portuguese milk. Protein and fat content varies depending on the type of milk. In this factory, and following the Portuguese consumption trends, 18% of the total milk produced is whole milk (including cocoa milk), 70% is semi-skimmed milk and 12% is skimmed milk. Whole milk contains 3.3% of protein and 3.9% of fat, semi-skimmed milk contains 3.5% of protein and 1.5% of fat and finally, skimmed milk contains 3.5% of pro- tein and 0.3% of fat. According to the formula defined by Bartl et al. (2011), taking into account that the lactose content is 4.8% regardless of the type of milk, it was defined that 1 kg of ECM is equivalent to 1.319 kg of UHT milk (18% whole, 70% semi-skimmed and 12% skimmed milk).

12: The dairy factory under assessment is a typical multi-output sys- tem (Fantin et al., 2012; Flysjö et al., 2011) where different types of UHT milk are produced: simple milk (whole, semi-skimmed and skimmed) and cocoa milk, as well as butter and cream (UHT and pasteurised). It was not possible to identify the corresponding mass and energy flows for each product due to the lack of information. Thus, the whole system was considered as a black box with three main groups of co-products: UHT milk (simple and cocoa), cream and butter. Therefore, allocation of the environmental burdens was needed in this study. The basis allocation method considered in this study was mass allocation since the main goal of the plant is the pro- duction of the milk, but the other co-products have to be produced in parallel. However, the assessment has been completed with two addi- tional allocation approaches: economic and protein content based al- locations in order to discuss the effect of the allocation procedure on the environmental results. When economic allocation was consid- ered, dairy market prices for the different co-products were taken into account. Table 1 shows a detailed description of the allocation factors, dairy products' prices and protein content.

The subsystem related with the dairy farm presents two main products: milk and meat. According to Castanheira et al. (2010), an economic allocation approach was applied in order to partition the environmental loads between both products. To do so, this allocation was done based on historical market prices for milk and meat in Por- tugal from 2002 to 2004 (Castanheira et al., 2010). The manure was not considered as a co-product since it has no economic value for the dairy farms. However, its use as organic fertiliser was considered for the production of agricultural inputs on the dairy farm such as maize and ryegrass.

The production line has been divided in two main subsystems (Fig. 1): the dairy farm (subsystem 1) and the dairy factory (subsystem 2).

Subsystem 1 - dairy farm: a well-managed and typical farm located in Portugal was considered in order to produce the raw milk re- quired in the milk factory. The characteristics of this farm are com- mon to dairy farms that produce most of the milk in the country

The raw milk is delivered from the dairy farm by road in tankers, and these are washed in the reception place using cleaning in place (CIP) processes. The raw milk is stored under cold conditions in stor- age tanks for the upcoming activities. The next step is the pre- warming followed by the skimming in order to separate the cream from the milk by a centrifugation process taking into consideration the characteristics of the final products. The second thermal milk treatment is the UHT treatment which allows increasing the life of the milk up to 6 or 9 months until opened and the destruction of almost all the bacteria still alive. The UHT treat- ment consists of heating the milk for a short period of time, around 2- 3 S at a high temperature (145 °C) and followed by a rapid cooling at 20 °C (room temperature). This thermal shock destroys the bacteria. It is important to point out that for the UHT cocoa milk production, the milk is previously mixed with the other ingredients (i.e., choco- late). After this thermal treatment, the final products are packaged in tetra-briks. This process takes place automatically in filling ma- chines and under sterilised conditions at room temperature. The production of cleaning agents, fuel, electricity and packages, as well as the treatment of waste and wastewater generated in the pro- duction line was included here. This dairy factory presents a wastewater treatment plant (WWTP) within the factory. This WWTP presents the classic scheme for these types of industries. This WWTP consists on an equalization/homogenization tank, an activated sludge tank with a sec- ondary clarifier, a sludge dewatering and a disinfection unit of the treat- ed effluent.
As mentioned previously, the production of capital goods was ex- cluded from the assessment.

Transport activities required for the delivery of the inputs to both subsystems were included within the system boundaries. On the con- trary, the delivery of final products from the dairy factory to whole- sale and retailers was excluded from the system boundaries as well as the consumption phase of the different products.

As in other studies, cleaning agents required in this subsystem for disinfection were not considered (Castanheira et al., 2010; Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000) due to the lack of data. It is important to highlight that the production of capital goods (machinery and buildings) and road infrastructures was excluded from the study (Berlin, 2002; Berlin et al., 2008; Bartl et al., 2011), as well as land use and soil quality changes caused by cultivation re- lated activities (Hogaas Eide, 2002). 
Subsystem 2 - dairy factory: this subsystem includes all the activities that take place in the dairy factory, from the raw milk reception to 
packaging and storage. The dairy factory under assessment is characterised by a large experience in milk production, using modern and automated processes and equipments in accordance with several BATs identified in the sector (Agência Portuguesa do Medio Ambiente, 2012). 
The raw milk is delivered from the dairy farm by road in tankers, and these are washed in the reception place using cleaning in place (CIP) processes. The raw milk is stored under cold conditions in stor- age tanks for the upcoming activities. The next step is the pre- warming followed by the skimming in order to separate the cream from the milk by a centrifugation process taking into consideration the characteristics of the final products. 7: <header>2.5. Inventory analysis </header>

When it was not possible to get primary data, inventory data were collected from literature and the Ecoinvent database, as described below. The same database has been used in order to achieve consis- tency in the results.

The information regarding the foreground data for the production of the raw milk (the main input for the UHT milk production) in a typical Portuguese dairy farm (subsystem 1) was obtained from Castanheira et al. (2010) taking also into account the production of the different inputs to (feed production) and outputs from (products, co-products and de- rived emissions) the subsystem and were completed with literature when necessary (Gallego et al., 2011). Derived emissions include air emissions such as CH4 from enteric fermentation and, N20 and NH3 emissions from manure management as well as water emissions such as NO3 leaching also from manure management.

Moreover, carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions from livestock respira- tion and CO2 sequestration by crops were not taken into account be- cause they are considered to be neutral for global warming potential (Bartl et al., 2011).

The foreground LCI data for dairy factory (subsystem 2) consisted of average annual data obtained by on-site measurements for the year 2008. Table 2 shows the detailed global inventory data collected for this subsystem.
Concerning the transport modes and average transport distances, Table 3 shows a short description of both.

Information concerning background processes such as cleaning agents, fossil fuel and packaging materials production were obtained from the Ecoinvent database and bibliographic sources. Table 4 shows a detailed description of the data sources considered.

Electricity requirements were taken directly from the Portuguese grid. The electricity production profile was adapted to the production model provided by the International Energy Agency (IEA, 2009) for Portugal. The energy sources used for the Portuguese electricity mix were fossil fuels (54%), such as hard coal, oil and natural gas,

The high contributions from the farm subsystem to the environ- mental performance are due to some specific and identified factors

(Castanheira et al., 2010). The production of the animal feed (mainly concentrates, maize silage and grass silage) was the main contributor from this subsystem in terms of ADP (26% of total) because of energy requirements in the related agricultural activities. Airborne and waterborne emissions derived from farm activities were responsible for 91% of GHG emissions derived from this subsystem, which amounted to 0.97 kg CO2eq per kg of ECM. The largest contributing emissions from dairy farm activities were CH4, N20 and CO2, in accor- dance with previous studies (Hogaas Eide, 2002; Berlin, 2002; de Boer, 2003; Casey and Holden, 2005; Flysjö et al., 2011), although the emission flows depend on how emissions are divided between the farm co-products, milk and meat (Casey and Holden, 2005; Flysjö et al., 2011). Special attention must be paid to the CH4 emitted from enteric fermentation and manure management (45% of total). This result fits in with other related studies concerning milk produc- tion under different management practices and locations (Haas et al., 2001; Berlin, 2002; Hospido et al., 2003; de Boer, 2003; Casey and Holden, 2005; de Vries and de Boer, 2010). According to Cederberg and Mattsson (2000) and de Boer (2003), the enteric CH4 emission in ruminants is highly dependent on the part of roughage in their fodder and digestibility, as well as on animal size and type. N20 emissions derived from manure management and from synthetic fertilisers application were also important (8% of total). Finally, fossil CO2 emissions from diesel combustion in agricultural machineries for the production of the animal feed, as well as from energy require- ments production in the dairy farm, are responsible for 7% of total GHG emissions.

For AP and EP, the farm subsystem dominates the milk production chain related contributions, with its contribution ranging from 81% in AP to 90% in EP. Concerning AP, about 90% of the contribution from this subsystem was due to ammonia (NH3) losses from manure. These are in accordance with the results from other authors (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000; Hogaas Eide, 2002; de Boer, 2003; Thomassen et al., 2008; Bartl et al., 2011; Fantin et al., 2012). nutrients from manure and fertilisers application (57% and 32% respectively of contribution from dairy farm to EP). The remarkable contribution from both emissions to EP was also identified by several authors where not only raw milk production at farms was assessed (Castanheira et al., 2010; Bartl et al., 2011), but also dairy products (Hogaas Eide, 2002; Berlin, 2002; Fantin et al., 2012).

With regard to ODP and POFP, the agricultural subsystem is respon- sible for 50% and 40% respectively of the total contributions to these im- pact categories. Diesel consumption at the dairy farm and combustion emissions derived from agricultural activities involved in the produc- tion of the animal feed are the main processes responsible for the con- tributing emissions to ODP. According to Cederberg and Mattsson (2000), Hogaas Eide (2002) and Berlin (2002), the cooling equipment on dairy farms could contribute to this impact category depending on the cooling media considered, since not all the media deplete ozone. Ex- amples of cooling media could be based on hydrochlorofluorocarbons or ammonia. In order to maintain the same assumptions as Cederberg and Mattsson (2000), the cooling media was not included within the system boundaries due to the complexity, the incomplete understand- ing of the process and the lack of information. Therefore, it should be noted that the contributions to ODP could be higher depending on the cooling media considered. The use of ammonia as the cooling media should not have contributions to ODP but its leakage could have effects in terms of AP and EP (Berlin, 2002).

Concerning POFP, the farm subsystem is an important contributor total) due to the CH4 emission from enteric fermentation in the cattle and manure management. A similar result was reported by Hospido et al. (2003) in their environmental study concerning pack- aged milk production.

With regard to the toxicity related categories, only some authors have considered their evaluation when dairy products are assessed (Berlin, 2002). According to the results of this study, the farm subsystem is re- sponsible for approximately 54% of total contributions to toxicity related categories, ranging from 37% in HTP and 94% in TEP. The production of the animal feed (mainly the concentrates) is the main factor responsible for these contributions due to metal related emissions derived from die- sel requirements and commercial fertilisers use.
The farm subsystem was also responsible for 42% of total CED due to the production of the animal feed (mainly concentrates and maize silage).

The remarkable contribution from both emissions to EP was also identified by several authors where not only raw milk production at farms was assessed (Castanheira et al., 2010; Bartl et al., 2011), but also dairy products (Hogaas Eide, 2002; Berlin, 2002; Fantin et al., 2012).

With regard to ODP and POFP, the agricultural subsystem is respon- sible for 50% and 40% respectively of the total contributions to these im- pact categories. According to Cederberg and Mattsson (2000), Hogaas Eide (2002) and Berlin (2002), the cooling equipment on dairy farms could contribute to this impact category depending on the cooling media considered, since not all the media deplete ozone. Therefore, it should be noted that the contributions to ODP could be higher depending on the cooling media considered. 11: <header>3.3. Environmental results derived from dairy factory </header>

Fig. 3 displays the relative contributions to each environmental category from the processes involved within the limits of the dairy factory (subsystem 2), but excluding the raw milk production (subsystem 1) and, therefore, the derived contributing flows, which have been previously analysed.

According to the results, several processes can be considered as key factors depending on the impact category, namely on-dairy pro- cesses (i.e., emissions generated on-site at the dairy factory) and off-dairy processes (e.g., Tetra-briks, energy requirements and trans- port related activities).

The largest responsible of ADP was the production of energy re- quirements at the dairy by means of fossil fuel (35%) for the heating sys- items and electricity for the different equipment involved in the milk treatments (21%). The production of the packages which are delivered from Spain is also a remarkable hot spot in terms of ADP (20%), as well as transport related activities (19%). The results obtained for CED are very similar to those of ADP, according to Fig. 3. Hospido et al. (2003) identified the Tetra-brik manufacture as the key factor in both impacts due to the production of the paper and natural gas requirements.

In terms of AP, on-site emissions derived from milk processing at the dairy factory were responsible for 72% of acidifying emissions. Emission of SO2 contributed to 63% of total emissions mainly derived from the boilers heating systems. Concerning EP, on-site emissions are also the major contribution (57%), followed by far for electricity and packages production (13% and 11% respectively). Nitrogen emis- sions to water added to 33% derived from the WWTP.

The contribution of processes involved in the dairy factory to GWP was mainly due to the on-site derived emissions (69%), especially due to the CO2 emitted from energy consumption. Transport related activ- ities, as well as electricity production, are also important contributors with contributions of 12% and 11% respectively.

In terms of ODP, the production of the fossil fuel required in the heating boilers contributed to 40% of the total emissions, followed by the delivery of materials to the factory (26%) and electricity re- quirements production (18%).

Concerning toxicity related categories, the off-site processes are the hot spots. Tetra-brik production is the major factor responsible for tox- icity emissions with contributions of 67%, 41%, 34% and 50% for HTP, FEP, MEP and TEP respectively. Electricity production contributions add from 11% in HTP to 34% in MEP. With regard to cleaning agents pro- duction, the contributions range from 11% in HTP to 23% in TEP. It is im- portant to notice that not all the authors have addressed in their studies the assessment of toxicity categories, most of the time due to the lack of available information (Thomassen et al., 2008, 2009). These impact cat- egories are mainly influenced by heavy metals emissions, and the pro- duction of fossil fuels required in the different off-site processes associated with the milk production were the major sources of heavy metals together with the polyethylene and aluminium production re- quired in the Tetra-brik manufacture, as well as the production of chemicals required for the cleaning agents. Finally, POFP impacts were mainly due to the on-site emissions (83%) and CO and SO2 emissions from boilers added up to 83% of total contributing emissions.

15: In this study, the LCA of packaged UHT milk including simple (whole, semi-skimmed and skimmed) and cocoa milk was assessed in detail from a cradle-to-gate perspective with the intention of iden- tifying the environmental hot spots. According to the results shown in Figs. 2 and 3, several involved processes widely contribute to the global environmental impact. By far the largest contributor to the

Fig. 3. Relative contribution to each impact category (in %) of each process involved in subsystem 2 considering mass allocation approach. Impact category acronyms: ADP=abiotic depletion, AP = acidification, =eutrophication, GWP = global warming, ODP = ozone layer depletion, =human toxicity, FEP = fresh water aquatic ecotoxicity, MEP=ma rine aquatic ecotoxicity, TEP = terrestrial ecotoxicity, POFP = photo-oxidants formation and CED = cumulative energy demand.

environmental profile is the production of the raw milk at the dairy farm. This result is not surprising since similar results have been found in other related studies where packaged milk was also evaluat- ed (Hogaas Eide, 2002; Hospido et al., 2003; Fantin et al., 2012).

The production of raw milk at the dairy farm is an issue that has been studied in detail for several years, considering different management practices such as conventional (intensive and extensive) and organic production in different countries such as Sweden (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000; Flysjö et al., 2011), Germany (Haas et al., 2001; Flysjö et al., 2011), Ireland (Casey and Holden, 2005), The Netherlands (Thomassen et al., 2008), New Zealand (Basset-Mens et al., 2009), Por- tugal (Castanheira et al., 2010), France (Williams et al., 2006; van der Werf et al., 2009) or Peru (Bartl et al., 2011).

However, remarkable variations were identified between our study and all these studies as well as between them, which will be discussed in this section. When LCA studies are compared, system boundaries and the functional unit must be the same (Casey and Holden, 2005; Yan et al., 2011). Despite these, considerable differences can be attribut- ed to issues such as different yields of animal or milk, different manage- ment practices at the farm level (organic, conventional), used databases or different co-product handling since the farm subsystem is a multi-output system (milk and meat production). Therefore, the selec- tion of the functional unit, the system boundaries, the allocation meth- od and other assumptions give uncertainty to the comparisons. Besides, the availability of good quality data for the assessment is another im- portant key factor (Thomassen et al., 2009).

As mentioned before, the raw milk production at the dairy farm was the main factor responsible for environmental effects in numer- ous impact categories. AP, EP and GWP are baseline impact categories considered in all the different studies regarding milk and dairy prod- ucts assessed. For this reason, the comparison of our results with re- lated studies has focussed on these impact categories.

The main impacts derived from raw milk production at the farm gate are mostly derived from on-farm emissions to air such as CH4 from enter- ic fermentation and manure management as well as NH3 from manure and fertiliser application. Moreover, emissions from fertiliser's applica- tion and manure management such as N20 (to air) and NO3 leaching (to soil) are also remarkable. The same achievements were obtained by other authors even considering different co-product handling methods (economic, mass and biological allocation or system expansion) and

management regimes at dairy farm. Table 6 shows a short detailed description of some of the results presented in related papers in terms of GWP, AP and EP. As shown in this table and in order to make the com- parison easy, the results obtained in the farm under study and initially expressed per kg of ECM at the dairy factory gate, have been expressed in other units commonly used by other authors: kg of ECM at farm gate, kg of milk at farm gate and kg of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) at farm gate. It is important to highlight that the results at that point are only affected by the allocation approach considered for the partitioning of the environmental burdens between the co-products at the dairy farm: raw milk and meat. Therefore, in this comparison the dairy factory subsystem was not included. According to the fat and pro- tein content of the raw milk at the dairy farm gate under study, one kg of average milk corresponded to ~0.99 kg FPCM. This calculation was based following the formula reported by CVB (2000).

According to the results shown in Table 6, our study found some re- markable differences with other production systems. With respect to GWP, when the results are shown per kg of milk, a similar value was obtained between our study (0.73 kg CO2eq) and Hospido et al. (2003), who reported 0.71 kg CO2eq. Hospido et al. (2003) centred their study in Galicia (NW Spain) which is close to Portugal and there- fore no large differences could be expected between them due to similar geographic conditions and agricultural management practices. In our study, the foreground inventory data for the farm activities were taken from Castanheira et al. (2010) whereas the background data were taken from the Ecoinvent database as well as information concerning alfalfa production (for the concentrates) was taken from Gallego et al. (2011). Castanheira et al. (2010) reported 1.0 kg CO2eq per kg milk, i.e., the GHG emissions were 27% higher. This value was close to the results reported by Basset-Mens et al. (2009), Cederberg and Mattsson (2000), Williams et al. (2006) and Fantin et al. (2012) for dairy exploitations located in New-Zealand, Sweden, the United Kingdom and Italy. Moreover, large differences were found in compar- ison to Haas et al. (2001) and Casey and Holden (2005). Differences on the data sources considered could explain the results as well as dif- ferences on assumed the characterisation factors (specifically in com- parison with the environmental results reported by Castanheira et al., 2010). When the results are shown per kg of ECM (Table 6), 0.75 kg CO2eq was reported in our study and almost the double (1.3-1.5 kg CO2eq) by Casey and Holden (2005). Differences in the allocation

approaches, on the content of fat and protein in the milk, which takes into account in the definition of the unit (kg ECM), as well as on the characterisation factors for the GHG emissions could explain the re- trieved differences. Finally, when the results are presented in terms of kg of FPCM, 0.74 kg CO2eq were obtained in our study, a value really low in comparison with published studies concerning milk production in The Netherlands (Thomassen and de Boer, 2005; Thomassen et al., 2008, 2009) and France (van der Werf et al., 2009), where up to 1.4 and 1.2 kg CO2 were obtained respectively under conventional man- agement. As explained above these differences could be explained by the use of different characterisation factors, allocation approaches and fat/protein content. In this study, it has been considered in the defini- tion of one kg FPCM, an average milk density of 1.03 kg/L (Casey and Holden, 2005) and that 1 kg of average milk corresponded to 0.99 kg FPCM (0.96 FPCM/L) according to the fat and protein content in the raw milk at the Portuguese dairy farm under assessment. However, Basset-Mens et al. (2009) reported that 1.09 kg FPCM per kg of milk and, van der Werf et al. (2009) and Thomassen and de Boer (2005) as- sumed that 1 kg FPCM corresponded to 1 L of milk. Therefore, and in the absence of information by these authors concerning the used fac- tors, part of these differences could explain the results.

The comparison between different production systems producing similar products requires a high degree of accuracy for inventory data, the usage of the same characterisation factors and a good and precise system boundaries description. One of the main reasons for these dif- ferences regardless of the considered functional unit is the agricultur- al management regime, since organic production involves higher CH4 emissions and lower N20 and CO2 emissions in comparison with con- ventional production (Haas et al., 2000; de Boer, 2003; Thomassen et al., 2008; Van der Werf et al., 2009) due to the lower milk production level per cow and increased use of roughage. On the contrary, Cederberg and Mattsson (2000) and Haas et al. (2001) reported higher GHG emissions for conventional than organic management. Moreover, it is be interesting to point out that in all the studies, CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation were mainly responsible for GHG emissions from farm activities (Fantin et al., 2012). Thomassen et al. (2008) stated that the content of roughage and starch in the an- imal feed considerably influenced the results. Castanheira et al.

(2010) assumed 109 kg CH4 per cow and year, and Hospido et al. (2003) considered 123 kg CH4 per cow and year. Thomassen et al. (2008) distinguished between organic (128 kg CH4 per cow and year) and conventional (113 kg CH4 per cow and year) systems and, Haas et al. (2001) contemplated 155 kg CH4 per cow and year. There- fore, the enteric fermentation and manure management are other remarkable aspects to be taken into account in the analysis and dis- cussion of different results.

With regard to the environmental results in terms of AP and EP, similar results were obtained between our system and compared sys- tems regardless the country and the unit (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000; Haas et al., 2001; Kanyarushoky et al., 2008; Castanheira et al., 2010; Fantin et al., 2012). However, the results obtained by Hospido et al. (2003) in both impact categories were considerably dif- ferent (79% and 49% lower) in comparison with other studies, which could be due to differences in the characterisation factors. Moreover, van der Werf et al. (2009) reported a reduction of 44% in terms of AP and an increment of 20% in EP in comparison with our study, both under conventional practices. The calculation of the NO3 and NH3 losses from manure and fertiliser application could affect these results.

Moreover, a flow indicator such as the energy use (in MJ) has also been considered due to the relevance of this indicator in several stud- ies (Hospido et al., 2003; Thomassen and de Boer, 2005; Thomassen et al., 2008; Basset-Mens et al., 2009; de Vries and de Boer, 2010). In our study, the energy use per kg of milk at farm gate was 3.8 MJ for a conventional and typical Portuguese dairy farm. This result is higher than in the majority of the studies where conventional dairy farms were assessed. Hospido et al. (2003) reported around 1.4 MJ per kg in Spain. Haas et al. (2001) and Williams et al. (2006) estimat- ed around 2.6 MJ per kg for German and English dairy farms respec- tively. Basset-Mens et al. (2009) in their study concerning milk production in New Zealand estimated between 0.6 and 1.6 MJ per kg milk. This result is really low since New Zealand's dairy farms used to be the ones with lowest inputs (such as fertilisers) and highest efficiencies in the world, as well as due to the climatic condi- tions. On the contrary, Thomassen et al. (2008) estimated up to 5.5 MJ per kg milk in Dutch conventional farms. It is expected that
differences in the databases considered and average milk production per cow influence the results.

The results obtained in our study for the production of the pack- aged milk at the dairy factory gate were compared with studies where the same product was assessed (Hospido et al., 2003; Kanyarushoky et al., 2008; Fantin et al., 2012), some differences may be identified between them according to Fig. 4. These differences are derived from methods of calculation (i.e. characterisation factors), milk production regimes, conversion factors (e.g. densities), sources of inventory data and allocation approaches. However and keeping in mind these differences, it can be noted that for EP and GWP, the re- sults from our study are situated in the range of values reviewed. Therefore, the results in this study could be considered useful as benchmark values to be used in future studies of the dairy sector in Portugal. Concerning the AP (Fig. 4a), the result obtained in this study is considerably higher in comparison with the others, which is due to the differences previously mentioned.

(2008) distinguished between organic (128 kg CH4 per cow and year) and conventional (113 kg CH4 per cow and year) systems and, Haas et al. (2001) contemplated 155 kg CH4 per cow and year. (2003) in both impact categories were considerably dif- ferent (79% and 49% lower) in comparison with other studies, which could be due to differences in the characterisation factors. The calculation of the NO3 and NH3 losses from manure and fertiliser application could affect these results.

approaches, on the content of fat and protein in the milk, which takes into account in the definition of the unit (kg ECM), as well as on the characterisation factors for the GHG emissions could explain the re- trieved differences. Finally, when the results are presented in terms of kg of FPCM, 0.74 kg CO2eq were obtained in our study, a value really low in comparison with published studies concerning milk production in The Netherlands (Thomassen and de Boer, 2005; Thomassen et al., 2008, 2009) and France (van der Werf et al., 2009), where up to 1.4 and 1.2 kg CO2 were obtained respectively under conventional man- agement. As explained above these differences could be explained by the use of different characterisation factors, allocation approaches and fat/protein content. In this study, it has been considered in the defini- tion of one kg FPCM, an average milk density of 1.03 kg/L (Casey and Holden, 2005) and that 1 kg of average milk corresponded to 0.99 kg FPCM (0.96 FPCM/L) according to the fat and protein content in the raw milk at the Portuguese dairy farm under assessment. However, Basset-Mens et al. (2009) reported that 1.09 kg FPCM per kg of milk and, van der Werf et al. (2009) and Thomassen and de Boer (2005) as- sumed that 1 kg FPCM corresponded to 1 L of milk. The comparison between different production systems producing similar products requires a high degree of accuracy for inventory data, the usage of the same characterisation factors and a good and precise system boundaries description. One of the main reasons for these dif- ferences regardless of the considered functional unit is the agricultur- al management regime, since organic production involves higher CH4 emissions and lower N20 and CO2 emissions in comparison with con- ventional production (Haas et al., 2000; de Boer, 2003; Thomassen et al., 2008; Van der Werf et al., 2009) due to the lower milk production level per cow and increased use of roughage. On the contrary, Cederberg and Mattsson (2000) and Haas et al. (2001) reported higher GHG emissions for conventional than organic management. Moreover, it is be interesting to point out that in all the studies, CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation were mainly responsible for GHG emissions from farm activities (Fantin et al., 2012). Thomassen et al. (2008) stated that the content of roughage and starch in the an- imal feed considerably influenced the results. Castanheira et al.

(2010) assumed 109 kg CH4 per cow and year, and Hospido et al. (2003) considered 123 kg CH4 per cow and year. Thomassen et al. (2008) distinguished between organic (128 kg CH4 per cow and year) and conventional (113 kg CH4 per cow and year) systems and, Haas et al. (2001) contemplated 155 kg CH4 per cow and year. There- fore, the enteric fermentation and manure management are other remarkable aspects to be taken into account in the analysis and dis- cussion of different results.

With regard to the environmental results in terms of AP and EP, similar results were obtained between our system and compared sys- tems regardless the country and the unit (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000; Haas et al., 2001; Kanyarushoky et al., 2008; Castanheira et al., 2010; Fantin et al., 2012). However, the results obtained by Hospido et al. (2003) in both impact categories were considerably dif- ferent (79% and 49% lower) in comparison with other studies, which could be due to differences in the characterisation factors. Moreover, van der Werf et al. (2009) reported a reduction of 44% in terms of AP and an increment of 20% in EP in comparison with our study, both under conventional practices. The calculation of the NO3 and NH3 losses from manure and fertiliser application could affect these results.

Moreover, a flow indicator such as the energy use (in MJ) has also been considered due to the relevance of this indicator in several stud- ies (Hospido et al., 2003; Thomassen and de Boer, 2005; Thomassen et al., 2008; Basset-Mens et al., 2009; de Vries and de Boer, 2010). In our study, the energy use per kg of milk at farm gate was 3.8 MJ for a conventional and typical Portuguese dairy farm. This result is higher than in the majority of the studies where conventional dairy farms were assessed. Hospido et al. (2003) reported around 1.4 MJ per kg in Spain. Haas et al. (2001) and Williams et al. (2006) estimat- ed around 2.6 MJ per kg for German and English dairy farms respec- tively. Basset-Mens et al. (2009) in their study concerning milk production in New Zealand estimated between 0.6 and 1.6 MJ per kg milk. This result is really low since New Zealand's dairy farms used to be the ones with lowest inputs (such as fertilisers) and highest efficiencies in the world, as well as due to the climatic condi- tions. On the contrary, Thomassen et al. (2008) estimated up to 5.5 MJ per kg milk in Dutch conventional farms. It is expected that
differences in the databases considered and average milk production per cow influence the results.

The results obtained in our study for the production of the pack- aged milk at the dairy factory gate were compared with studies where the same product was assessed (Hospido et al., 2003; Kanyarushoky et al., 2008; Fantin et al., 2012), some differences may be identified between them according to Fig. 4. These differences are derived from methods of calculation (i.e. characterisation factors), milk production regimes, conversion factors (e.g. densities), sources of inventory data and allocation approaches. However and keeping in mind these differences, it can be noted that for EP and GWP, the re- sults from our study are situated in the range of values reviewed. Therefore, the results in this study could be considered useful as benchmark values to be used in future studies of the dairy sector in Portugal. Concerning the AP (Fig. 4a), the result obtained in this study is considerably higher in comparison with the others, which is due to the differences previously mentioned.

In this study, it has been considered in the defini- tion of one kg FPCM, an average milk density of 1.03 kg/L (Casey and Holden, 2005) and that 1 kg of average milk corresponded to 0.99 kg FPCM (0.96 FPCM/L) according to the fat and protein content in the raw milk at the Portuguese dairy farm under assessment. However, Basset-Mens et al. (2009) reported that 1.09 kg FPCM per kg of milk and, van der Werf et al. (2009) and Thomassen and de Boer (2005) as- sumed that 1 kg FPCM corresponded to 1 L of milk. Therefore, and in the absence of information by these authors concerning the used fac- tors, part of these differences could explain the results.

The comparison between different production systems producing similar products requires a high degree of accuracy for inventory data, the usage of the same characterisation factors and a good and precise system boundaries description. One of the main reasons for these dif- ferences regardless of the considered functional unit is the agricultur- al management regime, since organic production involves higher CH4 emissions and lower N20 and CO2 emissions in comparison with con- ventional production (Haas et al., 2000; de Boer, 2003; Thomassen et al., 2008; Van der Werf et al., 2009) due to the lower milk production level per cow and increased use of roughage. On the contrary, Cederberg and Mattsson (2000) and Haas et al. (2001) reported higher GHG emissions for conventional than organic management. Moreover, it is be interesting to point out that in all the studies, CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation were mainly responsible for GHG emissions from farm activities (Fantin et al., 2012). Thomassen et al. (2008) stated that the content of roughage and starch in the an- imal feed considerably influenced the results. Castanheira et al.

(2010) assumed 109 kg CH4 per cow and year, and Hospido et al. (2003) considered 123 kg CH4 per cow and year. Thomassen et al. (2008) distinguished between organic (128 kg CH4 per cow and year) and conventional (113 kg CH4 per cow and year) systems and, Haas et al. (2001) contemplated 155 kg CH4 per cow and year. There- fore, the enteric fermentation and manure management are other remarkable aspects to be taken into account in the analysis and dis- cussion of different results.

With regard to the environmental results in terms of AP and EP, similar results were obtained between our system and compared sys- tems regardless the country and the unit (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000; Haas et al., 2001; Kanyarushoky et al., 2008; Castanheira et al., 2010; Fantin et al., 2012). However, the results obtained by Hospido et al. (2003) in both impact categories were considerably dif- ferent (79% and 49% lower) in comparison with other studies, which could be due to differences in the characterisation factors. Moreover, van der Werf et al. (2009) reported a reduction of 44% in terms of AP and an increment of 20% in EP in comparison with our study, both under conventional practices. The calculation of the NO3 and NH3 losses from manure and fertiliser application could affect these results.

Moreover, a flow indicator such as the energy use (in MJ) has also been considered due to the relevance of this indicator in several stud- ies (Hospido et al., 2003; Thomassen and de Boer, 2005; Thomassen et al., 2008; Basset-Mens et al., 2009; de Vries and de Boer, 2010). In our study, the energy use per kg of milk at farm gate was 3.8 MJ for a conventional and typical Portuguese dairy farm. This result is higher than in the majority of the studies where conventional dairy farms were assessed. Hospido et al. (2003) reported around 1.4 MJ per kg in Spain. Haas et al. (2001) and Williams et al. (2006) estimat- ed around 2.6 MJ per kg for German and English dairy farms respec- tively. Basset-Mens et al. (2009) in their study concerning milk production in New Zealand estimated between 0.6 and 1.6 MJ per kg milk. This result is really low since New Zealand's dairy farms used to be the ones with lowest inputs (such as fertilisers) and highest efficiencies in the world, as well as due to the climatic condi- tions. On the contrary, Thomassen et al. (2008) estimated up to 5.5 MJ per kg milk in Dutch conventional farms. It is expected that
differences in the databases considered and average milk production per cow influence the results.

The results obtained in our study for the production of the pack- aged milk at the dairy factory gate were compared with studies where the same product was assessed (Hospido et al., 2003; Kanyarushoky et al., 2008; Fantin et al., 2012), some differences may be identified between them according to Fig. 4. These differences are derived from methods of calculation (i.e. characterisation factors), milk production regimes, conversion factors (e.g. densities), sources of inventory data and allocation approaches. However and keeping in mind these differences, it can be noted that for EP and GWP, the re- sults from our study are situated in the range of values reviewed. Therefore, the results in this study could be considered useful as benchmark values to be used in future studies of the dairy sector in Portugal. Concerning the AP (Fig. 4a), the result obtained in this study is considerably higher in comparison with the others, which is due to the differences previously mentioned.

The production of raw milk at the dairy farm is an issue that has been studied in detail for several years, considering different management practices such as conventional (intensive and extensive) and organic production in different countries such as Sweden (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000; Flysjö et al., 2011), Germany (Haas et al., 2001; Flysjö et al., 2011), Ireland (Casey and Holden, 2005), The Netherlands (Thomassen et al., 2008), New Zealand (Basset-Mens et al., 2009), Por- tugal (Castanheira et al., 2010), France (Williams et al., 2006; van der Werf et al., 2009) or Peru (Bartl et al., 2011).

When LCA studies are compared, system boundaries and the functional unit must be the same (Casey and Holden, 2005; Yan et al., 2011). Despite these, considerable differences can be attribut- ed to issues such as different yields of animal or milk, different manage- ment practices at the farm level (organic, conventional), used databases or different co-product handling since the farm subsystem is a multi-output system (milk and meat production). Besides, the availability of good quality data for the assessment is another im- portant key factor (Thomassen et al., 2009).

As mentioned before, the raw milk production at the dairy farm was the main factor responsible for environmental effects in numer- ous impact categories. AP, EP and GWP are baseline impact categories considered in all the different studies regarding milk and dairy prod- ucts assessed. For this reason, the comparison of our results with re- lated studies has focussed on these impact categories.

The main impacts derived from raw milk production at the farm gate are mostly derived from on-farm emissions to air such as CH4 from enter- ic fermentation and manure management as well as NH3 from manure and fertiliser application. Moreover, emissions from fertiliser's applica- tion and manure management such as N20 (to air) and NO3 leaching (to soil) are also remarkable. Finally, when the results are presented in terms of kg of FPCM, 0.74 kg CO2eq were obtained in our study, a value really low in comparison with published studies concerning milk production in The Netherlands (Thomassen and de Boer, 2005; Thomassen et al., 2008, 2009) and France (van der Werf et al., 2009), where up to 1.4 and 1.2 kg CO2 were obtained respectively under conventional man- agement. As explained above these differences could be explained by the use of different characterisation factors, allocation approaches and fat/protein content. In this study, it has been considered in the defini- tion of one kg FPCM, an average milk density of 1.03 kg/L (Casey and Holden, 2005) and that 1 kg of average milk corresponded to 0.99 kg FPCM (0.96 FPCM/L) according to the fat and protein content in the raw milk at the Portuguese dairy farm under assessment. However, Basset-Mens et al. (2009) reported that 1.09 kg FPCM per kg of milk and, van der Werf et al. (2009) and Thomassen and de Boer (2005) as- sumed that 1 kg FPCM corresponded to 1 L of milk. Therefore, and in the absence of information by these authors concerning the used fac- tors, part of these differences could explain the results.

The comparison between different production systems producing similar products requires a high degree of accuracy for inventory data, the usage of the same characterisation factors and a good and precise system boundaries description. One of the main reasons for these dif- ferences regardless of the considered functional unit is the agricultur- al management regime, since organic production involves higher CH4 emissions and lower N20 and CO2 emissions in comparison with con- ventional production (Haas et al., 2000; de Boer, 2003; Thomassen et al., 2008; Van der Werf et al., 2009) due to the lower milk production level per cow and increased use of roughage. On the contrary, Cederberg and Mattsson (2000) and Haas et al. (2001) reported higher GHG emissions for conventional than organic management. Moreover, it is be interesting to point out that in all the studies, CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation were mainly responsible for GHG emissions from farm activities (Fantin et al., 2012). Thomassen et al. (2008) stated that the content of roughage and starch in the an- imal feed considerably influenced the results. Castanheira et al.

(2010) assumed 109 kg CH4 per cow and year, and Hospido et al. (2003) considered 123 kg CH4 per cow and year. Thomassen et al. (2008) distinguished between organic (128 kg CH4 per cow and year) and conventional (113 kg CH4 per cow and year) systems and, Haas et al. (2001) contemplated 155 kg CH4 per cow and year. There- fore, the enteric fermentation and manure management are other remarkable aspects to be taken into account in the analysis and dis- cussion of different results.

With regard to the environmental results in terms of AP and EP, similar results were obtained between our system and compared sys- tems regardless the country and the unit (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000; Haas et al., 2001; Kanyarushoky et al., 2008; Castanheira et al., 2010; Fantin et al., 2012). However, the results obtained by Hospido et al. (2003) in both impact categories were considerably dif- ferent (79% and 49% lower) in comparison with other studies, which could be due to differences in the characterisation factors. Moreover, van der Werf et al. (2009) reported a reduction of 44% in terms of AP and an increment of 20% in EP in comparison with our study, both under conventional practices. The calculation of the NO3 and NH3 losses from manure and fertiliser application could affect these results.

Moreover, a flow indicator such as the energy use (in MJ) has also been considered due to the relevance of this indicator in several stud- ies (Hospido et al., 2003; Thomassen and de Boer, 2005; Thomassen et al., 2008; Basset-Mens et al., 2009; de Vries and de Boer, 2010). In our study, the energy use per kg of milk at farm gate was 3.8 MJ for a conventional and typical Portuguese dairy farm. This result is higher than in the majority of the studies where conventional dairy farms were assessed. Hospido et al. (2003) reported around 1.4 MJ per kg in Spain. Haas et al. (2001) and Williams et al. (2006) estimat- ed around 2.6 MJ per kg for German and English dairy farms respec- tively. Basset-Mens et al. (2009) in their study concerning milk production in New Zealand estimated between 0.6 and 1.6 MJ per kg milk. This result is really low since New Zealand's dairy farms used to be the ones with lowest inputs (such as fertilisers) and highest efficiencies in the world, as well as due to the climatic condi- tions. On the contrary, Thomassen et al. (2008) estimated up to 5.5 MJ per kg milk in Dutch conventional farms. It is expected that
differences in the databases considered and average milk production per cow influence the results.

The results obtained in our study for the production of the pack- aged milk at the dairy factory gate were compared with studies where the same product was assessed (Hospido et al., 2003; Kanyarushoky et al., 2008; Fantin et al., 2012), some differences may be identified between them according to Fig. 4. These differences are derived from methods of calculation (i.e. characterisation factors), milk production regimes, conversion factors (e.g. densities), sources of inventory data and allocation approaches. However and keeping in mind these differences, it can be noted that for EP and GWP, the re- sults from our study are situated in the range of values reviewed. Therefore, the results in this study could be considered useful as benchmark values to be used in future studies of the dairy sector in Portugal. Concerning the AP (Fig. 4a), the result obtained in this study is considerably higher in comparison with the others, which is due to the differences previously mentioned.

The production of raw milk at the dairy farm is an issue that has been studied in detail for several years, considering different management practices such as conventional (intensive and extensive) and organic production in different countries such as Sweden (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000; Flysjö et al., 2011), Germany (Haas et al., 2001; Flysjö et al., 2011), Ireland (Casey and Holden, 2005), The Netherlands (Thomassen et al., 2008), New Zealand (Basset-Mens et al., 2009), Por- tugal (Castanheira et al., 2010), France (Williams et al., 2006; van der Werf et al., 2009) or Peru (Bartl et al., 2011).

However, remarkable variations were identified between our study and all these studies as well as between them, which will be discussed in this section. When LCA studies are compared, system boundaries and the functional unit must be the same (Casey and Holden, 2005; Yan et al., 2011). Despite these, considerable differences can be attribut- ed to issues such as different yields of animal or milk, different manage- ment practices at the farm level (organic, conventional), used databases or different co-product handling since the farm subsystem is a multi-output system (milk and meat production). Therefore, the selec- tion of the functional unit, the system boundaries, the allocation meth- od and other assumptions give uncertainty to the comparisons. Besides, the availability of good quality data for the assessment is another im- portant key factor (Thomassen et al., 2009).

As mentioned before, the raw milk production at the dairy farm was the main factor responsible for environmental effects in numer- ous impact categories. AP, EP and GWP are baseline impact categories considered in all the different studies regarding milk and dairy prod- ucts assessed. For this reason, the comparison of our results with re- lated studies has focussed on these impact categories.

The main impacts derived from raw milk production at the farm gate are mostly derived from on-farm emissions to air such as CH4 from enter- ic fermentation and manure management as well as NH3 from manure and fertiliser application. Moreover, emissions from fertiliser's applica- tion and manure management such as N20 (to air) and NO3 leaching (to soil) are also remarkable. The same achievements were obtained by other authors even considering different co-product handling methods (economic, mass and biological allocation or system expansion) and

management regimes at dairy farm. Table 6 shows a short detailed description of some of the results presented in related papers in terms of GWP, AP and EP. As shown in this table and in order to make the com- parison easy, the results obtained in the farm under study and initially expressed per kg of ECM at the dairy factory gate, have been expressed in other units commonly used by other authors: kg of ECM at farm gate, kg of milk at farm gate and kg of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) at farm gate. It is important to highlight that the results at that point are only affected by the allocation approach considered for the partitioning of the environmental burdens between the co-products at the dairy farm: raw milk and meat. Therefore, in this comparison the dairy factory subsystem was not included. According to the fat and pro- tein content of the raw milk at the dairy farm gate under study, one kg of average milk corresponded to ~0.99 kg FPCM. This calculation was based following the formula reported by CVB (2000).

According to the results shown in Table 6, our study found some re- markable differences with other production systems. With respect to GWP, when the results are shown per kg of milk, a similar value was obtained between our study (0.73 kg CO2eq) and Hospido et al. (2003), who reported 0.71 kg CO2eq. Hospido et al. (2003) centred their study in Galicia (NW Spain) which is close to Portugal and there- fore no large differences could be expected between them due to similar geographic conditions and agricultural management practices. In our study, the foreground inventory data for the farm activities were taken from Castanheira et al. (2010) whereas the background data were taken from the Ecoinvent database as well as information concerning alfalfa production (for the concentrates) was taken from Gallego et al. (2011). Castanheira et al. (2010) reported 1.0 kg CO2eq per kg milk, i.e., the GHG emissions were 27% higher. This value was close to the results reported by Basset-Mens et al. (2009), Cederberg and Mattsson (2000), Williams et al. (2006) and Fantin et al. (2012) for dairy exploitations located in New-Zealand, Sweden, the United Kingdom and Italy. Moreover, large differences were found in compar- ison to Haas et al. (2001) and Casey and Holden (2005). Differences on the data sources considered could explain the results as well as dif- ferences on assumed the characterisation factors (specifically in com- parison with the environmental results reported by Castanheira et al., 2010). approaches, on the content of fat and protein in the milk, which takes into account in the definition of the unit (kg ECM), as well as on the characterisation factors for the GHG emissions could explain the re- trieved differences. Finally, when the results are presented in terms of kg of FPCM, 0.74 kg CO2eq were obtained in our study, a value really low in comparison with published studies concerning milk production in The Netherlands (Thomassen and de Boer, 2005; Thomassen et al., 2008, 2009) and France (van der Werf et al., 2009), where up to 1.4 and 1.2 kg CO2 were obtained respectively under conventional man- agement. As explained above these differences could be explained by the use of different characterisation factors, allocation approaches and fat/protein content. In this study, it has been considered in the defini- tion of one kg FPCM, an average milk density of 1.03 kg/L (Casey and Holden, 2005) and that 1 kg of average milk corresponded to 0.99 kg FPCM (0.96 FPCM/L) according to the fat and protein content in the raw milk at the Portuguese dairy farm under assessment. However, Basset-Mens et al. (2009) reported that 1.09 kg FPCM per kg of milk and, van der Werf et al. (2009) and Thomassen and de Boer (2005) as- sumed that 1 kg FPCM corresponded to 1 L of milk. Therefore, and in the absence of information by these authors concerning the used fac- tors, part of these differences could explain the results.

The comparison between different production systems producing similar products requires a high degree of accuracy for inventory data, the usage of the same characterisation factors and a good and precise system boundaries description. One of the main reasons for these dif- ferences regardless of the considered functional unit is the agricultur- al management regime, since organic production involves higher CH4 emissions and lower N20 and CO2 emissions in comparison with con- ventional production (Haas et al., 2000; de Boer, 2003; Thomassen et al., 2008; Van der Werf et al., 2009) due to the lower milk production level per cow and increased use of roughage. On the contrary, Cederberg and Mattsson (2000) and Haas et al. (2001) reported higher GHG emissions for conventional than organic management. Moreover, it is be interesting to point out that in all the studies, CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation were mainly responsible for GHG emissions from farm activities (Fantin et al., 2012). Thomassen et al. (2008) stated that the content of roughage and starch in the an- imal feed considerably influenced the results. Castanheira et al.

(2010) assumed 109 kg CH4 per cow and year, and Hospido et al. (2003) considered 123 kg CH4 per cow and year. Thomassen et al. (2008) distinguished between organic (128 kg CH4 per cow and year) and conventional (113 kg CH4 per cow and year) systems and, Haas et al. (2001) contemplated 155 kg CH4 per cow and year. There- fore, the enteric fermentation and manure management are other remarkable aspects to be taken into account in the analysis and dis- cussion of different results.

With regard to the environmental results in terms of AP and EP, similar results were obtained between our system and compared sys- tems regardless the country and the unit (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000; Haas et al., 2001; Kanyarushoky et al., 2008; Castanheira et al., 2010; Fantin et al., 2012). However, the results obtained by Hospido et al. (2003) in both impact categories were considerably dif- ferent (79% and 49% lower) in comparison with other studies, which could be due to differences in the characterisation factors. Moreover, van der Werf et al. (2009) reported a reduction of 44% in terms of AP and an increment of 20% in EP in comparison with our study, both under conventional practices. The calculation of the NO3 and NH3 losses from manure and fertiliser application could affect these results.

Moreover, a flow indicator such as the energy use (in MJ) has also been considered due to the relevance of this indicator in several stud- ies (Hospido et al., 2003; Thomassen and de Boer, 2005; Thomassen et al., 2008; Basset-Mens et al., 2009; de Vries and de Boer, 2010). In our study, the energy use per kg of milk at farm gate was 3.8 MJ for a conventional and typical Portuguese dairy farm. This result is higher than in the majority of the studies where conventional dairy farms were assessed. Hospido et al. (2003) reported around 1.4 MJ per kg in Spain. Haas et al. (2001) and Williams et al. (2006) estimat- ed around 2.6 MJ per kg for German and English dairy farms respec- tively. Basset-Mens et al. (2009) in their study concerning milk production in New Zealand estimated between 0.6 and 1.6 MJ per kg milk. This result is really low since New Zealand's dairy farms used to be the ones with lowest inputs (such as fertilisers) and highest efficiencies in the world, as well as due to the climatic condi- tions. On the contrary, Thomassen et al. (2008) estimated up to 5.5 MJ per kg milk in Dutch conventional farms. It is expected that
differences in the databases considered and average milk production per cow influence the results.

The results obtained in our study for the production of the pack- aged milk at the dairy factory gate were compared with studies where the same product was assessed (Hospido et al., 2003; Kanyarushoky et al., 2008; Fantin et al., 2012), some differences may be identified between them according to Fig. 4. These differences are derived from methods of calculation (i.e. characterisation factors), milk production regimes, conversion factors (e.g. densities), sources of inventory data and allocation approaches. However and keeping in mind these differences, it can be noted that for EP and GWP, the re- sults from our study are situated in the range of values reviewed. Therefore, the results in this study could be considered useful as benchmark values to be used in future studies of the dairy sector in Portugal. Concerning the AP (Fig. 4a), the result obtained in this study is considerably higher in comparison with the others, which is due to the differences previously mentioned.

The allocation factor for the UHT milk (in its different forms: simple and cocoa milk) could change from 86.4% for mass alloca- tion, to 90.7% for protein-based allocation and to 56.5% for economic allocation due to the differences on the protein content and on the

The consideration of economic allocation should im- prove the environmental profile (in each category under assessment) for the UHT milk in 34.3%. On the contrary, the environmental profile should be up to 5% worse when protein-based allocation is assumed. Moreover, when mass allocation is considered, the UHT milk products that receive the highest environmental impact attributions are the semi-skimmed UHT milk (60.2%) and the skimmed milk (10.5%) since they are the main products in terms of production amount. 32: More than 90% of the milk consumed in Portugal is UHT milk, this being the most important dairy product consumed in that country. A large number of LCA studies based on dairy factories have been pub- lished to date, but have not focused on the Portuguese sector. For this reason, a dairy factory located in Portugal and representative of the state-of-art was evaluated in order to quantify its environmental im- pacts as well as to identify the hot spots. Moreover, the results were compared with other published studies. According to the results, the production of the raw milk in the dairy farm represents a hot spot in several categories under assessment. Main flows affecting the results are the CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation as well as N20 and NH3 emissions to air and leaching of NO3 to the water coming from ma- nure management and fertilisers production and application. In addi- tion, on-site emissions derived from the dairy factory (excluding from the assessment of the dairy farm) are important in terms of AP, EP, GWP and POFP. Tetra-brik production required for the milk packaging and energy requirements production are the main flows. When the environmental results were compared with published studies, the values obtained are in the range of other papers. However, differences were also identified due to several factors such as the allocation approach, data sources, characterisation factors, farm management regimes and enteric fermentation emission factors. Therefore, these aspects should be carefully addressed and sensitivity to the assumptions and uncertainty of the results should be evaluated when different studies are compared.
